does: This paper introduces AgentPoison, a backdoor attack method that targets LLM agents by poisoning their memory modules or retrieval-augmented generation knowledge bases. The core contribution is formulating trigger generation as a constrained optimization problem that creates backdoor triggers mapping poisoned instances to a distinct embedding space. When a user instruction contains the optimized trigger, the agent retrieves malicious demonstrations from the poisoned knowledge base, causing it to execute unintended actions or produce harmful outputs. The method is evaluated against both memory-based and RAG-based agents across multiple tasks, demonstrating that poisoning relatively small numbers of knowledge base entries can reliably trigger misbehavior while maintaining normal performance on clean inputs.
connects: This work builds on the expanding literature of adversarial attacks and backdoor vulnerabilities in large language models, extending prior work on prompt injection and jailbreaking to the agent setting. It responds to growing concerns about the safety of retrieval-augmented generation systems, which have become standard in modern LLM applications. The paper engages with embedding-space manipulation techniques from adversarial machine learning and connects to red-teaming methodologies developed for evaluating LLM robustness. It also relates to broader work on data poisoning attacks in machine learning and recent research on vulnerabilities in tool-use and API-calling capabilities of agents.
enables: This work enables future research into defending against backdoor attacks in agent systems, motivating the development of robust detection mechanisms for poisoned knowledge bases and memory modules. It points toward evaluation frameworks for assessing agent trustworthiness in realistic deployment scenarios where knowledge sources may be untrusted or compromised. The findings suggest the need for verification mechanisms at retrieval time and adversarial robustness techniques tailored to agent architectures. The work also enables exploration of poisoning attacks targeting other agent components like tool specifications or planning prompts, and supports the development of safety standards and certifications for agents operating with external knowledge sources in high-stakes applications.
